talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: University of Rochester, Wilmot Cancer Institute
talk_metadata.talk_title: NEAT1 paraspeckles, detained introns and ferroptosis sensitivity in pancreatic cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I can guarantee you that today, in every talk you'll see in this session, reverse transcriptase was used in almost every slide."

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: NEAT1 long isoform as paraspeckle scaffold
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted that NEAT1 knockout alone drives a basal-like, more aggressive phenotype, suggesting reliance on a single lncRNA scaffold that could be circumvented by targeting downstream pathways.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Focus on NEAT1 expression as a biomarker without a clear therapeutic agent; current work is limited to cell line and mouse models.

key_data_gaps: No in vivo tumor efficacy data presented
No patient outcome correlation beyond TCGA expression analysis

crispro_opportunity.description: Low NEAT1/paraspeckle levels define basal-like pancreatic tumors that are hypersensitive to GPX4 inhibitors (ferroptosis inducers).
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "If we just treat these basal-like cells with RSL3 straight up, the basal-like cells are indeed more sensitive to these drugs."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO could develop a predictive model linking NEAT1 expression signatures to ferroptosis drug response, enabling patient selection.

crispro_opportunity.description: Current GPX4 inhibitor trials lack NEAT1-based enrichment; a biomarker-driven arm could improve signal.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "NEAT1 knockout cells were sensitized to GPX4 inhibitors RSL3 and ML162."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO to simulate trial outcomes with NEAT1 stratification and optimize enrollment criteria.

unresolved_questions: What are the upstream signals that regulate NEAT1 expression in basal versus classical PDAC subtypes?
Do paraspeckle protein knockdowns phenocopy NEAT1 loss?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute / Harvard Medical School
talk_metadata.talk_title: ZC3H18 truncations drive ERV RNA accumulation and oncogenesis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I think as far as I'm aware, this is the second genetic cause of ERV elevation in cancer."
Speaker said: "We showed that Z18 trunc works in a dominant negative fashion on these RNAs."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Dominant‑negative truncations sequester ERV RNAs in the cytoplasm, bypassing nuclear decay machinery

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated that truncating mutations lose binding to the exosome helicase but retain binding to RNA‑stabilizing proteins, revealing a modular weakness.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: No therapeutic strategy currently exploits the dominant‑negative mechanism; reliance on genetic loss makes drug targeting challenging.

key_data_gaps: Lack of in‑human clinical correlation beyond expression analyses
No pharmacologic inhibitor of the ZC3H18‑ERV axis presented

crispro_opportunity.description: ZC3H18 truncations create a dominant‑negative protein that stabilizes ERV RNAs; CrisPRO could design small molecules or PROTACs to degrade the mutant protein.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "The truncated version lost binding to the very important parts of the next complex, the key helicase and adapter protein."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the mutant ZC3H18 structure and predict ligands that restore exosome recruitment or promote mutant degradation.

crispro_opportunity.description: ZC3H18 loss/deletion occurs in ~30% of cancers; ERV RNA up‑regulation could serve as a biomarker for patient selection for viral‑mimicry therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Deletion of this occurs in almost 30% of all cancers, mostly epithelial cancers."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO to integrate ERV expression signatures with ZC3H18 mutation status to define a predictive cohort.

cited_competitors.name: VHL loss
cited_competitors.context: Previously reported mechanism of ERV up‑regulation in cancer

unresolved_questions: What determines the specificity of ZC3H18 for ERV RNAs versus other nuclear surveillance substrates?
Can pharmacologic restoration of exosome recruitment reverse the oncogenic phenotype?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Splicing‑regulated LAG‑3 intron retention controls T‑cell exhaustion and enables conditional therapeutic payloads
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We identified two RNA binding proteins, at least one of which is upregulated itself upon T cell exhaustion, so appears to be one of the gene expression program of exhausted T cells."
Speaker said: "The pluses and minuses are you do discover a lot of interesting new RNA isoforms, obviously with the long read RNA‑seq as an example here."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Retention introduces an in‑frame stop codon, leading to NMD and loss of functional LAG‑3 protein on acutely stimulated T cells

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed the minigene construct and intron‑based switch, indicating the core IP is the synthetic intron design rather than a novel protein.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on chronic stimulation to activate the switch may limit efficacy in tumors with low antigen load.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that intron retention in LAG‑3 leads to loss of surface protein (observation) but concluded that LAG‑3 is a therapeutic target for checkpoint blockade (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: In vivo efficacy of the splicing‑regulated FOXO1/IL‑12 constructs beyond tumor growth curves
Safety assessment of chronic cytokine expression

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to identify small molecules that enhance the LAG‑3 intron removal, synergizing with the splicing‑regulated payloads.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We identified two RNA binding proteins, at least one of which is upregulated upon T cell exhaustion, that regulate the splicing of that intron in LAG3."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the splicing factor‑RNA interaction and predict modulators to boost intron excision.

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate that long‑read single‑cell RNA‑seq uniquely discovers functional intron retention events missed by short‑read data.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Even in our own short‑read deep RNA sequencing data, we don't see this differential splicing event. It's really only been seen now through the use of long‑read sequencing."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage CrisPRO's isoform‑resolution analytics to position the platform as essential for next‑gen immunotherapy target discovery.

cited_competitors.name: PD‑1/LAG‑3 antibodies
cited_competitors.context: Current FDA‑approved checkpoint inhibitors targeting LAG‑3

unresolved_questions: Which additional splicing factors besides the two identified control the LAG‑3 intron?
Can the intron‑based switch be generalized to other exhaustion‑associated genes?

assets_to_track: FOXO1 and IL‑12 engineered T‑cell therapies
